title: Chocolate cold-process soap
subtitle: Cocoa butter and cocoa powder give this bar a warm brown tone and a rich, creamy lather
excerpt: A cold-process bar built on cocoa butter, olive oil, and coconut oil, coloured and lightly scented with cocoa powder and sweet orange essential oil. After a 6-week cure the bar is firm, warm-brown, with a dense creamy lather and a faint chocolate scent.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: cocoa butter 0.137, olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; water as 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), oil property tables and cocoa butter soap guidance. USDA AOCS fatty acid profile, cocoa butter stearic and palmitic content. Cocoa powder as soap colourant documented in Catherine Failor, Making Natural Liquid Soaps (2000), notes on natural colourants.

recipe.batchNotes: Run any change to oil weights through a lye calculator before scaling. Adjust cocoa powder proportionally.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould before unmoulding. The bar benefits from a full 6-week cure; cocoa butter soaps can feel draggy on skin until fully cured.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store on an open rack in a cool dark place. Cured bars keep two years. Discard if orange oily spots (DOS) develop or the bar smells sharp and paint-like rather than faintly chocolatey.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between fat and sodium hydroxide (lye) that produces soap and glycerine. Each oil has a SAP value: the grams of lye required to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The percentage of fat left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5% superfat, the bar retains a small reserve of free oil for conditioning.
The point at which soap batter thickens to a custard consistency and a drizzle on the surface briefly holds its shape. The cue to pour into the mould.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out the loaf after 2 days. The soap should be firm but may still feel slightly soft at the centre. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves.

projectSchedule.title: Turn bars
projectSchedule.body: Flip each bar. The brown cocoa colour deepens as moisture leaves. There may be a faint bloom of lighter colour on the surface; this is normal and fades.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: A fully cured chocolate bar is hard, dark brown, and mild on the skin. The faint cocoa scent persists in the cured bar.

content.text: Cocoa butter is one of the hardest soap-making fats, contributing stearic acid that gives the finished bar density and longevity. This formula uses cocoa butter as 25% of the oil weight, with olive oil for conditioning and coconut oil for lather and cleansing. Unsweetened cocoa powder, added at
trace
, colours the batter a warm mid-brown and leaves a faint chocolate note in the cured bar. Sweet orange essential oil lifts the cocoa scent without competing with it. At 5%
superfat
and a full 6-week cure, this is a rich, firm bar suited to normal-to-dry skin. Yield is 7 bars of around 130 g each.
Before starting, put on protective gloves and goggles.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 25% of oil weight; chop if in solid block form
30% of oil weight; melt if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat on this blend
3% of oil weight; a clean citrus note that lifts the cocoa scent

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a ventilated space with gloves and goggles on. Weigh the sodium hydroxide into a small container. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream, stirring constantly. Never add water to lye. The solution heats rapidly and gives off fumes; keep your face away from the steam. Leave the pitcher somewhere safe to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Melt and prepare the oils

Combine the cocoa butter and coconut oil in a heatproof bowl. Melt gently over low heat until liquid. Remove from the heat and stir in the olive oil and castor oil. Sift the cocoa powder into a small bowl. Pre-mix it with a teaspoon of the oil blend to form a smooth paste; this prevents clumping in the batter. Allow the oil blend to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Combine and blend to trace

Pour the lye solution into the oil blend in a slow, steady stream. Use the stick blender in short 5-second bursts, alternating with hand stirring, until the batter reaches light trace. Stir in the cocoa paste and blend for another 10 seconds. If using sweet orange essential oil, add it now and blend briefly. The batter will be a warm dark-brown colour. Pour immediately.
Pour and insulate
Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Smooth the surface with the spatula. Wrap in a towel and leave undisturbed for 48 hours. The
saponification
heat may cause a gel phase at the centre. Gelled soap shows as a darker, slightly translucent zone in the cut bar; it is not a defect and fades over the following weeks.
Unmould, cut, and cure

After 2 days, turn out the loaf. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves; the bars are still mildly caustic at this stage. Arrange on a rack with space around each bar. Cure in a cool, dry room out of sunlight for 4 to 6 weeks. Cocoa butter soaps benefit from the full 6-week cure; they can feel slightly draggy on the skin before the stearic acid softens fully through use.
Storage and shelf life

Store cured bars on an open rack. The cocoa colour holds well in dark storage; direct light can fade it over several months. Fully cured bars keep two years. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots (DOS) or a sharp smell that does not resemble cocoa.
